content.text: Monday afternoon. A cooling breeze, a gloomy sky, and I am tired. We got married three days ago and my wife had to fly to Bangkok in the morning. I put my AirPods in, I am not in the mood for a conversation. I duck into my Grab and tell the driver I might fall asleep quickly.

He makes small talk anyway, and somehow I play ball and reply, smiling. As we cross Singapore, the conversation moves from how much a Grab driver makes, to whether that is enough to raise children, to why Singapore needs immigration to prop up its fertility rate.
I am curious. I check online. Singapore is at 0.87 in 2025, below the replacement rate, the lowest resident births in its recorded history.

I am at the age where conversations around kids keep happening. Lately the news has been full of it too: a baby-formula recall, a fight about folate and autism, a government promising to “make our children healthy again.” So when I got home, I did the thing I always do. I pulled five years of Google Trends and went looking for what the world actually wants to know about feeding its children.
That conversation with the Grab driver turned up a first surprise.
The fewer the babies, the louder the search

Singapore barely makes babies. Yet for every child it does have, it searches about feeding them harder than almost anywhere on earth. That surprised me, so I plotted every country I could find against its fertility rate.

At first the raw map looks almost sensible: the countries having the most babies search breastfeeding the most, as a share of everything they look up, with Kenya, Indonesia, Egypt and Nigeria near the top. Then I divided each country’s interest by its actual number of births, and it inverted. Singapore rose to the top, beside Ireland, Finland and Slovenia, tiny, wealthy and barely reproducing, while India and Nigeria sank to the floor. Switch the topic and the leaders move, the United States tops baby formula, the United Kingdom tops infant feeding, but per child the pattern holds every time. The fewer the babies, the louder the search.

attrs.headline: Search attention is anxiety, not headcount

content.text: The loudest markets are not the ones having the most children. They are the wealthy, low-fertility ones where each birth is rarer, more deliberate, and more heavily invested in. Fewer babies means more scrutiny per baby, and that scrutiny shows up as search.

So a search graph for early-life nutrition is not a map of where children are. It is a map of where parents worry, compare and spend. That was the cross-section. The next surprise came when I watched it move.
Then I watched it over time

I lined the same topics up across five years. For most of that stretch, almost nothing happens; the lines sit flat, year after year. Then, from the middle of 2025, the picture changes. A handful of worries climb hard while the rest stay put.

Breastfeeding is still far and away the most-searched topic, but it has barely moved in five years. What surges instead is a set of newer worries: stage-specific nutrition (infant, toddler, early-childhood) off a small base, iron and vitamin D deficiency, children’s brain development, folic acid, children’s vitamins. What parents worry about is changing faster than how much they worry.

The sharpest mover is infant and toddler nutrition: flat for four years, then up more than fivefold in nine months. And it did not climb smoothly. It jumped in three steps, three waves, each one a piece of American news, even though the everyday searching is worldwide.
Wave one: the first jump (August 2025)

Wave one is August 2025. We have already seen the headline jump, infant and toddler nutrition up fivefold. But which of the broad, everyday topics moved with it? Almost none. Breastfeeding and baby food stayed flat. Only baby formula broke trend.
Could it just be the calendar? Each topic peaks in its own month, and formula’s is May, not August. A spike in formula searches in August is out of season. This is news, not a holiday.

Two stories had collided. The White House released its “Make Our Children Healthy Again” strategy, putting childhood nutrition and ultra-processed food on every front page, while a run of lawsuits over the marketing of “toddler milks” pushed those exact terms into the conversation.
Wave two: the safety scares (November 2025 and January 2026)

The second wave was fear. In early November 2025 the American brand ByHeart recalled every batch of its infant formula after dozens of babies fell ill with botulism. Interest in the brand spiked roughly thirtyfold in a single week, then collapsed. The signature of a reputational shock, not of demand.

Then it went global, and back to my driver’s part of the world. In January 2026, Nestlé, Danone and Lactalis pulled products in more than sixty countries after a toxin called cereulide was traced to a single Chinese supplier. Aptamil, Cow & Gate and SMA recalled from Europe to Asia; French prosecutors opening a probe; Nestlé posting its biggest search month in five years. Two recalls, two months apart, one local, one global, and the graph caught both.
Wave three: folate and autism (spring 2026)

The third wave was science, or an argument about it. Through spring 2026 an American policy push tied prenatal folate and acetaminophen to autism, and floated a folate derivative as a treatment. Searches for folic acid and prenatal vitamins went to record highs.

Folic acid roughly tripled between February and May, with no comparable rise in any earlier spring. Prenatal vitamins hit an all-time peak. Even the niche “breastfeeding diet” rode the wave. One caveat the piece owes you: the folate-autism link is scientifically contested, and the advice to take folic acid in early pregnancy long predates this news cycle. The science did not change. The conversation did, and search moved with it almost in real time.
How the brands reacted
Three news shocks in nine months. Did the companies selling to these parents see them coming?

The contrast is instructive. Bobbie spent the period building goodwill: Cardi B as its “Chief Confidence Officer,” campaigns with athletes about the pressure of motherhood, a “Formula Is Food” transparency message. So when a rival’s recall sent anxious parents looking for alternatives, it had the attention and the supply to catch them. Danone leaned into emotional support, a French campaign around postpartum mental health and a 24/7 helpline. The incumbents, Abbott and Reckitt, spent the window on the defensive, shadowed by litigation and clean-label scrutiny.
You can see it in the search itself. Track the brands, and most sit flat until a recall yanks one of them up for a week.

ByHeart is a vertical line in November, a thirtyfold spike that vanishes as fast as it came. Aptamil jumps with the January recall. Only Bobbie rises on purpose, climbing all through 2025 on marketing alone. One caveat: this is share of attention, not share of market, who is talked about, not who is bought. But the difference between a brand that grows and a brand that merely spikes is written right there in the lines.
What another brand can take from this
You do not have to sell formula for this to be useful. Any brand exposed to seasonal cycles, safety scares or shifting health stories can read these eighteen months as a playbook.
On crisis

Speed and transparency decide who keeps the customers. Regulators called ByHeart’s recall too slow. A rival’s recall spikes anxiety across the whole category, not just the brand at fault, so the brands ready with stock, search visibility and honest “here is how we test” content caught the parents it lost; the ones who paused to work it out did not. The moment is an opportunity, but only if you are ready before it arrives. The one thing never to do is feed the fear: formula marketing sits under strict codes, and reassurance has to be about your own standards, never about trashing a rival.
On promotion and growth

Seasonality is a gift if you respect the lead time. Intent builds before it peaks, so you start about three months ahead of a seasonal high, not during it. For breastfeeding products, ramp from May towards August. For children’s vitamins, October for January, June for back to school.

Geography rewards tailoring. Folate and autism is an American conversation; heavy metals and clean labels, largely American too; formula recalls went global. Match the message to the worry that is live where you spend. Premium, low-fertility markets (Korea, Singapore, Israel, Switzerland) reward ingredient science and premium positioning, because each child is a rarer, higher-value customer. America rewards agility, with budget held back for the next news moment. The high-birth markets that barely register in English need their own languages and price tiers, not a translated premium campaign.

And a wave is not growth. A recall spike is borrowed attention; it leaves as fast as it came. The brand that compounds is the one, like Bobbie, building its own reasons to be searched between the shocks.
What search can, and can’t, tell you

A few honest limits. Search is attention, not sales. The monthly geographic data is noisy at the edges. And the science under the loudest wave is disputed, so the honest posture is to report what people searched, not to judge whether they were right to.

But step back, and the bigger picture is hard to miss. It brings me back to the Grab. The driver was right: fewer children are coming, across most of the wealthy world, and emphatically in his own country. Yet every one of those rarer children now arrives into more scrutiny, more choice and more marketing than any before. The search graph is that paradox made visible: a small, shrinking subject that, for one strange and noisy year, the whole internet could not stop asking about.
The countries having the fewest babies are the ones searching hardest about how to feed them, and in 2025–26, the news taught all of them to search even harder.
